Games/F1 Pit Stop VR
PrototypeGame DesignVR ProgrammingInteraction Design

F1 Pit Stop VR

A rhythm-based VR pit stop simulation. Synchronize your wheel gun maneuvers with the beat to secure bolts with mechanical precision — blending professional racing maintenance with addictive rhythmic flow.

Watch Trailer ↗← All Games

Highlights

Rhythm-based VR interaction: synchronize actions (unscrew, pull, push) with timed beats

Bilateral validation — both hands must act as a unified input source to trigger actions

Temporal thresholding — filters out high-frequency noise, requiring 50ms sustained velocity

Arc teleportation with NavMesh validation and Niagara VFX (long-distance teleportation disabled for better experience)

Trailer

The Idea

Experience a high-octane F1 pit stop in this rhythm-based VR simulation. Synchronize your wheel gun maneuvers with the beat to secure bolts with mechanical precision. This game blends professional racing maintenance with addictive rhythmic flow for a unique and satisfying pit crew challenge. The core insight was that pit stops are already rhythmic — real crews drill the same sequence thousands of times until it becomes muscle memory. By mapping that to VR hand tracking with rhythm game timing, the physical gestures become the controller.

Core Gameplay Loop

Stand in the VR pit lane next to an F1 car. A colour-coded wheel mechanism guides your actions — the concentric rings show progress and timing windows. Three action types, each mapped to a physical gesture: 1. Unscrew — Press the trigger button at the correct point; the screw flies onto the wheel gun's head. 2. Pull — Both hands simultaneously touch the tire and lift upwards; the tire flies along a set trajectory driven by spline curves. 3. Push — Both hands simultaneously touch the tire and push forward; the tire follows its trajectory back onto the axle. An array-based keypoint system controls the choreography — each entry defines {MovementType, time, tolerance}, dictating what action to take at what second and how much margin of error is allowed. Hold both grip buttons to start. Complete the sequence as fast and accurately as possible.

Two-handed pull gesture lifting the tyre

Two-handed push gesture replacing the tyre

Unscrew action with trigger timing

Design Notes

Actions

Pull Action

The Pull action uses a timeline driving a spline-based trajectory: Get Spline Length → Lerp between 0 and spline length → Get Location at Distance Along Spline → Set Relative Location + Rotation on the tyre component. The animation curve ramps linearly for the pull, creating a smooth lift-off.

Push Action

The Push action reverses this process: timeline plays the spline trajectory in reverse, then triggers a sequence — Stop Pull → Set Relative Location (reset tyre) → Set Relative Rotation (reset) → Set Visibility on bolt → Set Actor Location (final position).

Detection Pipeline

The detection pipeline runs on Event Tick: Get Component Location → Subtract Vector → Threshold Check → Boolean AND → Branch Node → Increment Counter → Validation Check → IsValid Index? → Collision Check → Trigger Movement

Key Safeguards

Two key safeguards: • Temporal Thresholding — An Index > Time check filters out high-frequency noise and unintentional twitches, requiring a minimum of 50ms sustained velocity. • Bilateral Validation — Synchronicity checks ensure that both hands act as a unified input source, eliminating asymmetrical misfires and reinforcing the physical "weight" of the interaction.

Teleportation

Teleportation uses a satisfying arc trace — green for valid NavMesh destinations, red with an X for invalid spots. Long-distance teleportation was disabled for a better gaming experience, keeping the player focused on the pit station.

Art & Feel

The F1 car sits in a stylised pit garage with bold red walls and orange/black livery. The wheel mechanism uses concentric rings with colour segments (red outer ring, blue/green/yellow inner ring) providing immediate visual feedback. Wall-mounted text prompts ("unscrew", "pull", "success") appear during gameplay, reinforcing the rhythm-game feel.

Lessons & Iterations

01

What surprised me: The rhythm-based approach made the pit stop feel more like a dance than a chore. Players naturally fell into a flow state after a few attempts.

02

What I cut: A full pit crew simulation with multiple stations. Focused on perfecting the single-tyre interaction and the feel of the rhythm timing.

03

What I'd improve: Add haptic feedback patterns for each action type and more varied rhythm patterns across difficulty levels.

Big Brother

A retro horror adventure where a watchful eye hunts you through pixelated corridors. Outsmart its patrol routes, dodge its traps, and escape — or be caught.

Roll for Resolution

A sci-fi strategy game where you play as an alien Mediator racing to save Earth from an apocalyptic threat. Negotiate with divided global powers, draft tactical cards, manage the Doom Clock, and roll dice to overcome unique national traits and secure world peace.

  • 10+ card types across 4 categories: Basic Stats, Dice Manipulation, Risk & Special, Universal Mechanics
  • National Trait system with mathematical formulas affecting gameplay (Strict, PanicProne, FixedDie, Elitist)
  • Doom Clock — failed negotiations advance it; game ends when the hour hand completes a full rotation
